keyPoints: Simplifying Algebraic Fractions: Factorise numerator and denominator fully, then cancel common factors
Improper Algebraic Fractions: Use algebraic long division so the degree of numerator < degree of denominator
Partial Fractions (Distinct Linear Factors): Split into the form A/(x-a) + B/(x-b)
Partial Fractions (Repeated Linear Factors): Split into the form A/(x-a) + B/(x-a)² + C/(x-b)
Finding Constants in Partial Fractions: Substitute suitable values of x to eliminate terms and solve
Equating Coefficients: Expand the identity and equate coefficients of corresponding powers of x
Remainder Theorem: When polynomial f(x) is divided by (ax-b), the remainder is f(b/a)
Factor Theorem: If f(a) = 0, then (x-a) is a factor of the polynomial f(x)
Mappings and Functions: A mapping is a function only if every valid input has exactly one output
Domain and Range: Domain is the set of all inputs x, Range is the set of all outputs f(x)
Composite Functions: fg(x) means apply function g first, then apply function f to the result
Inverse Functions: f⁻¹(x) exists if and only if f(x) is a one-to-one function
Finding Inverses: Swap x and y in the equation, then rearrange to make y the subject
Graph of Inverse: The graph of y = f⁻¹(x) is a reflection of y = f(x) in the line y = x
Modulus Function: |x| = x for x ≥ 0, and |x| = -x for x < 0
Graph of y = |f(x)|: Reflect any parts of the curve below the x-axis upwards in the x-axis
Graph of y = f(|x|): Erase the left side of the y-axis, then reflect the right side in the y-axis
Solving Modulus Equations: Isolate the modulus expression, then split into positive and negative cases
Secant Function: sec θ = 1 / cos θ
Cosecant Function: cosec θ = 1 / sin θ
Cotangent Function: cot θ = 1 / tan θ = cos θ / sin θ
Pythagorean Identities: 1 + tan²θ ≡ sec²θ and 1 + cot²θ ≡ cosec²θ
Addition Formula (Sine): sin(A ± B) = sin A cos B ± cos A sin B
Addition Formula (Cosine): cos(A ± B) = cos A cos B ∓ sin A sin B
Addition Formula (Tangent): tan(A ± B) = (tan A ± tan B) / (1 ∓ tan A tan B)
Double Angle (Sine): sin 2A = 2 sin A cos A
Double Angle (Cosine): cos 2A = cos²A - sin²A = 2cos²A - 1 = 1 - 2sin²A
R-alpha Form: a cos θ ± b sin θ = R cos(θ ∓ α) where R = √(a² + b²) and tan α = b/a
Derivative of Natural Exponential: d/dx(eˣ) = eˣ
Derivative of Scaled Exponential: d/dx(eᵏˣ) = k eᵏˣ
Derivative of Natural Logarithm: d/dx(ln x) = 1/x
Derivative of General Base: d/dx(aˣ) = aˣ ln a
Exponential Growth Model: N = A eᵏᵗ where k > 0 models an increasing population
Exponential Decay Model: N = A e⁻ᵏᵗ where k > 0 models a decreasing quantity
Log-linear Graphs: Plotting ln y against t for y = A eᵏᵗ produces a straight line with gradient k
Log-log Graphs: Plotting log y against log x for y = A xⁿ produces a straight line with gradient n
Chain Rule: dy/dx = (dy/du) × (du/dx) used for composite functions
Product Rule: d/dx(uv) = u(dv/dx) + v(du/dx) used for multiplied functions
Quotient Rule: d/dx(u/v) = (v(du/dx) - u(dv/dx)) / v² used for divided functions
Differentiating Sine: d/dx(sin kx) = k cos kx
Differentiating Cosine: d/dx(cos kx) = -k sin kx
Differentiating Tangent: d/dx(tan kx) = k sec²(kx)
Differentiating Secant: d/dx(sec x) = sec x tan x
Differentiating Cosecant: d/dx(cosec x) = -cosec x cot x
Differentiating Cotangent: d/dx(cot x) = -cosec²x
Implicit Differentiation: Differentiate y terms with respect to y, then multiply by dy/dx
Integrating Exponentials: ∫ eᵏˣ dx = (1/k)eᵏˣ + C
Integrating Reciprocals: ∫ (1/x) dx = ln|x| + C
Integrating Fractions f'/f: ∫ (f'(x)/f(x)) dx = ln|f(x)| + C
Integrating Sine: ∫ sin kx dx = -(1/k)cos kx + C
Integrating Cosine: ∫ cos kx dx = (1/k)sin kx + C
Integrating Secant Squared: ∫ sec²kx dx = (1/k)tan kx + C
Integration by Substitution: Replace x with u to simplify the integral, remembering to convert dx to du
Integration by Parts: ∫ u (dv/dx) dx = uv - ∫ v (du/dx) dx
Volume of Revolution (x-axis): V = π ∫ y² dx evaluated between limits a and b
Volume of Revolution (y-axis): V = π ∫ x² dy evaluated between limits c and d
Position Vector: A vector from the origin O to a point A, denoted as a or OA
Vector Magnitude: Length of vector a = xi + yj + zk is |a| = √(x² + y² + z²)
Unit Vector: A vector of length 1 in the direction of a, calculated as a / |a|
Vector Equation of a Line: r = a + λb where a is a position vector and b is a direction vector
Parallel Lines: Two lines are parallel if their direction vectors are scalar multiples of each other
Intersecting Lines: Found by equating the r vectors of two lines and solving for scalars λ and μ
Skew Lines: Lines in 3D space that are neither parallel nor intersect
Dot Product (Scalar Product): a · b = a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + a₃b₃
Angle Between Vectors: Calculated using cos θ = (a · b) / (|a| |b|)
Perpendicular Vectors: Two non-zero vectors are perpendicular if their dot product a · b = 0
Formulating Differential Equations: Express a contextual rate of change mathematically using dy/dt or dx/dt
Direct Integration: If dy/dx = f(x), then the solution is y = ∫ f(x) dx
Separation of Variables: Rearrange dy/dx = f(x)g(y) to the form ∫ (1/g(y)) dy = ∫ f(x) dx
General Solution: The integrated equation containing an arbitrary constant of integration + C
Particular Solution: Found by substituting boundary or initial conditions into the general solution to evaluate C
Proportional Rates: 'Rate of growth is proportional to y' translates to dy/dt = ky
Newton's Law of Cooling: Rate of temperature change is proportional to difference with surroundings: dT/dt = -k(T - T₀)
Connected Rates of Change: Use the chain rule to link related variables, e.g., dy/dt = (dy/dx) × (dx/dt)
